title: Freedom Score
subtitle: Data by [Freedom House](#dod:freedom-house). The score captures the extent of free and fair elections, political pluralism and participation, a functioning government, freedom of expression and association, the rule of law, and personal autonomy. Higher scores indicate more freedom.
caption:
Freedom Score, 2025
Data by Freedom House. The score captures the extent of free and fair elections, political pluralism and participation, a functioning government, freedom of expression and association, the rule of law, and personal autonomy. Higher scores indicate more freedom.

This choropleth map displays the 2025 Freedom Score for countries worldwide, using a continuous color scale ranging from pale yellow for the lowest scores to deep navy blue for the highest, with a maximum value of 101. The data visualizes the global distribution of civil liberties and political rights, where darker shades represent higher levels of freedom. The highest values, falling in the 80 to 101 band, are concentrated across North America, including the United States and Canada, as well as Western and Northern Europe, encompassing nations such as the United Kingdom, France, Germany, and the Nordic countries. Australia and Japan also appear in this top tier. Moving down the scale, countries in the 60 to 80 range include major economies like India and Brazil, alongside nations in Southern Africa and parts of Southeast Asia. The middle bands, representing scores between 40 and 60, cover significant portions of Latin America, Eastern Europe, and parts of Asia. Lower scores, indicated by pale green and yellow hues corresponding to the 20 to 40 range and below, are visible across much of the Middle East, Central Asia, and large areas of sub-Saharan Africa. Russia and China are situated in the lowest bands on the scale. Greenland and a few other territories are marked with diagonal hatching to indicate no data is available. The geographic pattern shows a clear clustering of high freedom scores in the Americas and Western Europe, while lower scores dominate across Asia and Africa.
